content: Maybe the guys over at Rive can put together some SVG converting library. They already have a proprietary (and open source) vector engine for Flutter. @luigi-rosso

Why not to add support importing svg into Flutter project. And convert to dart code/new vector drawable etc. Note that the same situation is on native Android, that's why we don't use svg directly (which I agree does not make much sense) and import to project instead, svg is stripped from not supported and not needed functionalitites and converted into android path definition.

Isn't that good idea for Flutter?
@giaur500 This issue isn't about that. If you have a suggestion specifically about SVG, please file a separate issue.
To be fair, many of the comments in this issue are discussing SVG and Flutter.

#follow 

SVG is perfect for great pixel-level user interface. Properly the best so far for great pixels.
@lukepighetti Yes. They are unfortunately all off-topic and having them here will not result in any improvements to Flutter.

> From earlier discussion, some important considerations are:
> 
> * We don't want full SVG support.  There is too much in the spec that is expensive, heavyweight, and/or duplicates what we already have in Flutter.
> * We should emphasize in our supported format those operations are effective/efficient/optimized in our graphics engine (Skia).
> * We may want to process the format at build time rather than support a full run-time interpreter.

A build time tool would be great, take the vector and render all the screen densities, and add the assets as images. Apples iOS does this with PDF icons.

> Better to be able to import svg and process them internally, either at build time or auto generate code during import. Similar as native Android - you can import svg and Android path definition xml will be generated from source svg. Import into Flutter project could generate dart code to be reused to draw vector asset.

i like it

> @giaur500 This issue isn't about that. If you have a suggestion specifically about SVG, please file a separate issue.

New issue has been created: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/63752

So what's the state of this?
Is there any intention at all of supporting something similar to VectorDrawable, or even VectorDrawable itself for those on Android?

As a developer considering adopting the ecosystem, I'd say this is a strong point that'd convince me to stay native..

I never tried it myself, but flutter svg lib used to have support for at
least key features of vector drawable like paths.

On Sat, 15 Aug 2020, 19:46 n00bmind, <notifications@github.com> wrote:

> So what's the state of this?
> Is there any intention at all of supporting something similar to
> VectorDrawable, or even VectorDrawable itself for those on Android?
>
> As a developer considering adopting the ecosystem, I'd say this is a
> strong point that'd convince me to stay native..
>
> —
> You are receiving this because you were mentioned.
> Reply to this email directly, view it on GitHub
> <https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/1831#issuecomment-674375846>,
> or unsubscribe
> <https://github.com/notifications/unsubscribe-auth/ABI4Y4LWYSYEIWI3RRSSCK3SAZKO5ANCNFSM4B3FXMMQ>
> .
>
I guess state is nothing. No conclusion exists here from this thread and also  my idea to develop something similar to native vector drawable closed immediately.
Is there any reason why the flutter svg package won't work for you? We are using it with great success in one of our apps. I have never used Vector Drawable so I'm not sure how it compares to SVG.

It seems the flutter_svg package is worth a look for this kind of use case..
@lukepighetti how would you rate its performance? My usage would be just a splash image and a few icon-sized graphics here and there in the app, so nothing fancy.

> It seems the flutter_svg package is worth a look for this kind of use case..
> 
> @lukepighetti how would you rate its performance? My usage would be just a splash image and a few icon-sized graphics here and there in the app, so nothing fancy.

I would rate it very high for your use case. I think people should really give it a try before they put pressure on the flutter team to do something internally.

@lukepighetti 
> before they put pressure on the flutter team to do something internally.

Real problem with this particular issue is that Skia the flutter graphics engine has [everything needed](https://skia.org/user/api/SkPath_Overview) to make "drawable-like" vector format running at the highest speed without importing quite heavy dart package that calls engine to draw primitives step by step.

> I think people should really give it _(svg package)_ a try

I think that people should loudly and in every possible place call Googlers to this gh-issue, because now — after four years — only negative PR may kick someone's at Google to think about whether mobile framework in year 2020 should really be forcing us to carry all that megabytes of raster graphics within our apps.

[@Hixie]

Unless I'm missing something, the implementation for such a flutter feature would look a lot like @dnfield 's [flutter_svg](https://github.com/dnfield/flutter_svg) package which appears to solve the use case you are describing. On top of that, the maintainer is on the core Flutter team so we can be sure of its quality. Would you feel better if this package was in https://github.com/flutter/plugins ?

If there is something I'm missing please feel free to educate me so I can learn more about this topic.

@lukepighetti 

I think that "not SVG" in the issue's title should be made red, bold and flashing.

This issue is specifically *not* about svg format.

OP (and me) had hopes for exposing Skia's path api so the assets graphics could be represented in a compact form - ie binary of fixed endianess. Dan even did a [patch to Skia](https://github.com/flutter/engine/pull/4957) that works for the flutter_svg, which in turn now allows us to use svg assets. Though still we parse xml at app run time and draw by calling the engine — also for the static assets bundled in the apk.

> With proper support for the *custom vector drawable format* (lets name it **fvd**), parsing of designer work would happen once — at app build time, then object in *fvd* format would be thrown directly at Skia. In graphics-heavy app such approach would have enormous effect on the app size and noticeable savings on rendering time. [*citing self*]

I hope that above shows how I see this issue and justifies my call to have it at last done.
Even as a part of the flutter_svg library.

Flutter deserves to have yet another advantage over competing technologies.

For what it's worth, there's nothing inherently wrong with using flutter_svg, it's just that it's lacking even some very simple things, e.g. [it doesn't support percentages](https://github.com/dnfield/flutter_svg/issues/103) and straight up crashes when trying to render them. This comment is not a knock on the flutter_svg library, it's just to explain to people who are saying we can just use flutter_svg.

flutter_svg seems work very well despite of not support scripting, HTML, SMIL etc.

 it's looks good and we can build SVG from files assets, network or ordinary strings.
my problem with flutter_svg is, that it only supports android and ios. I think it is crucial to have svg in web and native as well.

SVG images are no longer supported in web from today new beta version.
We really need a solution to this, I can't find a proper way to use svg images in Flutter web.
flutter_svg works if you use the CanvasKit backend. Unfortunately, the html backend is missing a few features that flutter_svg depends on.
@dnfield what feature is missing, do you have an issue filed?.

The big blocker is `Canvas.drawPicture`, which has a bug here: https://github.com/flutter/flutter/issues/48491

Things more or less work on the CK backend though.

> flutter_svg works if you use the CanvasKit backend. Unfortunately, the html backend is missing a few features that flutter_svg depends on.

Yes but we can't really force the mobile browsers to use CanvasKit, as it is way more heavy.

can't we just add an kIsWeb switch?

Francesc Arolas <notifications@github.com> schrieb am Sa., 6. Feb. 2021,
14:34:

> flutter_svg works if you use the CanvasKit backend. Unfortunately, the
> html backend is missing a few features that flutter_svg depends on.
>
> Yes but we can't really force de mobile browsers to use CanvasKit, as it
> is way more heavy.
>

> can't we just add an kIsWeb switch?

Mobile browsers are Web as well so no. Anyways it is unrelated, you can compile your app to use CanvasKit on web always, but as I said, it is way more heavy.
It should be possible to fix the missing feature

@ohir commented on Apr 24, 2018:
> There is a compact [format for subset of svg](https://godoc.org/golang.org/x/exp/shiny/iconvg) coined for _Shiny_ (Go desktop framework) by someone at the big **G**. Its binary representation and can bring default Android launcher icon size under 100 bytes.

Hi. I'm the "someone at the big G" who invented Shiny and its vector graphics file format, IconVG. I've only just seen this issue.

IconVG is a compact, binary format for simple vector graphics: icons, logos, glyphs and emoji.

IconVG is similar in concept to SVG (Scalable Vector Graphics) but much simpler. **Compared to "SVG Tiny", it does not have features for text, multimedia, interactivity, linking, scripting, animation, XSLT, DOM, combination with raster graphics such as JPEG formatted textures, etc.**

Quoting myself from the [IconVG announcement](https://groups.google.com/forum/#!topic/golang-nuts/LciLeI5p8k8):

"The SVG spec prints as 400 pages, not including the XML, CSS or XSLT specifications. [The [IconVG godoc.org page](https://godoc.org/golang.org/x/exp/shiny/iconvg), which includes the file format specification, prints as 26 pages]...

The Material Design icon set... consists of 961 vector icons. **As SVG files, this totals 312,887 bytes.** As 24 * 24 pixel PNGs, 190,840 bytes. As 48 * 48 pixel PNGs, 318,219 bytes. **As IconVGs, 122,012 bytes.**"

---

Some examples (in binary IconVG, "byte-code disassembled" (human-readable) IconVG, SVG and PNG) are at https://github.com/golang/exp/tree/master/shiny/iconvg/testdata

---

I'm happy to work with Flutter folks if they're interested in integrating it. Right now, I only know of a Go implementation, but I can write a C/C++ one if needed. The [Go package](https://github.com/golang/exp/tree/master/shiny/iconvg) (decoder, encoder and basic tests) weighs under 5k lines of code.
That seems like a match made in heaven.

@Hixie ping!

> The Material Design icon set... consists of 961 vector icons. **As SVG files, this totals 312,887 bytes.** As 24 * 24 pixel PNGs, 190,840 bytes. As 48 * 48 pixel PNGs, 318,219 bytes. **As IconVGs, 122,012 bytes.**"

> Hi. I'm the "someone at the big G" who invented Shiny and its vector graphics file format, IconVG. [...] I'm happy to work with Flutter folks if they're interested in integrating it. Right now, I only know of a Go implementation, but I can write a C/C++ one if needed. [@ nigeltao]

[cc: @dnfield]

IconVG looks interesting. I'm still working on determining what feature set we need to address here, and I suspect that our goals may differ slightly from IconVG's (e.g. animation and text, and batch-loading data for shader-based implementations are things I'm currently thinking we should support, which aren't supported by IconVG as far as I can tell), but to the extent that we can work together, I'm happy to do so.

IconVG is an extensible format, and animation was always possibly part of a version 0.2. I just haven't had the need for it yet.

Similarly, icon collections and sharing common data across multiple icons could be in a future version. I'm not sure if that's what you mean by batch data.

For shaders, https://github.com/reactivego/ivg is apparently a GPU-based IconVG renderer but I haven't looked deeply into it.

Text is trickier, if it requires pulling in a full OpenType rasterization and text shaping engine as dependencies, and some mechanism of embedding fonts (not just their 'flattened' vectors of the glyphs in use). One not-immediately-obvious design principle is that **IconVG is not an authoring format, only a presentation format**.

The analogy is that an artist can use Photoshop (and its PSD file format) with all its rich authoring, layering and typesetting tools, but will export to PNG for use in their app. Fluttter only has to ship a PNG decoder, not a PSD decoder. Similarly, artists could work in Illustrator (AI files) or Inkscape (SVG files) and export to IconVG. The authoring tools certainly need to know text, but the IconVG renderer doesn't necessarily need to.

Anyway, the more you let me know about your required feature set, the more I can say whether IconVG is (or can be) appropriate.

> programttically composable variants (think added star badge or similar)

Yeah, an IconVG graphic (see this [example disassembly](https://github.com/golang/exp/blob/master/shiny/iconvg/testdata/action-info.lores.ivg.disassembly)) consists of:

1. a magic identifier,
2. one or more metadata bytes then
3. a sequence of variable length instructions for a virtual machine. 

Adding a star badge should be as easy as concatenating the instructions, possibly also inserting a "re-initialize the icon-global state" op to e.g. reset the color registers to their initial values.

As I said, icon collections and sharing common data across multiple icons could be in a future version. This could mean that a star badge would be like a Combining Diacritical Mark in a font, where 'a' and 'acute' are separate glyphs (icons) that are combined to make 'a-acute'.

> IMO, it would be much more useful to support addressable layers within.

Addressable layers could also allow animation logic to be externalized, which I think would benefit use cases where vector animations are meant to coordinate with other motion in an application.
Isn't that basically Rive?

> Isn't that basically Rive?

Well i believe rive had to find some workarounds in the form of the rive package (much like flutter_svg) to make that work because the discusion about a fully engine integrated drawable format wasn't resolved  at the time.

5 years past.. Are there any news about support for SVG in flutter? I hope that they will make support from the box, not "Package". Besides of it, it is very difficult to add some custom font icons in project without using of standart material design icons.
@Mickey-A-Mouse This issue is specifically not about SVG (see the title); if you have SVG-specific needs, please file a separate issue.

Please correct me, if I'm wrong. The current standing with vector image formats in Flutter is the following:

1. Out of the box only Fonts are supported and allow drawing vector images. There're two problems with this format: only single or dual tone images can be displayed; one needs extra processing of input files (most likely SVGs) via 3rd party tool to prepare a font and also Dart classes (there're plug-ins on pub.dev)
2. There're plugins on pub.dev which can render static svgs, though there can be issue with stability(I believe flutter_svg didn't work with canvaskit) and platform support (not all plug-ins offer all 6 platforms), additionally many plug-ins are not updated to null-safety.

Long story short, want vector images - use Fonts or pub.dev plugins.

Besides, it is not quite clear what this ticket offers to do. Create own Flutter vector image format native to the platform and tooling to convert to it? Or do limited support of SVG or other vector image format to allow rendering of known types of vector images (and bypass complicated stuff such as animations, scripting, HTML embedding etc.)?

Btw, what are other wide spread vector image formats besides SVG.

> is not quite clear what this ticket offers to do. Create own Flutter vector image format native to the platform and tooling to convert to it?

This issue calls for exposing Skia's (flutter drawing engine, vector one) primitives via a simple compact binary format for the purpose of embedding scalable image assets. Once format (eg. proposed in 2016 IconVG) will be estabilished and supported by the engine then conversion tools may emerge from the community.

> Or do limited support of SVG or other vector image format to allow rendering of known types of vector images

SVG is supported via the pub package.

> SVG is supported via the pub package.

@ohir unfortunately using `flutter_svg` in production with lots of dynamic images is not very reliable. We often run into issues where some features of SVG aren't supported resulting in broken SVGs.

We even built workarounds where we send the SVG to a NodeJS server, convert the SVG to a PNG with the help of [puppeteer](https://github.com/puppeteer/puppeteer) to reliably display SVGs without any UI bugs.

However, I deeply appreciate all the work @dnfield puts into this package. 💪

The reason why the team is not supporting SVG might because that the full feature of SVG is good enough to support a UI rendering directly, rather than just imaging. But the design of flutter is too different than SVG, specially on web.

While this effort is very admirable, I do wonder if a completely new vector format on its own is going to help with what the majority of the +1's on this comment are for, which I'm guessing are mostly from people arrived at it after searching for "Vector Drawable" and clicking on the first result. What _I_ really need is a way to easily display icons & other simple graphics extracted from the files my designer sends me or that I draw in Inkscape etc. A new vector format is a good first step but realistically it wouldn't help me _that_ much, unless someone also made tooling to convert to that format from something more well-used.

The big advantage of VectorDrawable is that for simple SVGs you can just copy the majority of it over and have it more or less work as you'd expect. And for anything more complicated like advanced animations I'd try to avoid doing it in SVGs anyways.

What I've been doing so far in my apps is editing the SVGs myself to remove as many complicated/unnecessary features as possible, then running the resulting files through [SVGOMG](https://jakearchibald.github.io/svgomg/) to reduce the # of paths, and then running the resulting paths through a custom parser I've been slowly expanding based on https://github.com/matanlurey/svg that I use to spit out simple calls to the canvas, which I draw in a CustomPainter or occasionally a BoxPainter.

Eventually I'd like to build that out to use code generation such that I could drop a SVG in my source, run the build_runner (which would error out for unsupported features in the SVG, and maybe eventually generate images it compares), and then simply import it - with the caveat that the SVG had to be quite simple. While that goes completely against what this issue is about at this point, it seems much more useful to me...
I am also confused by the title change

@lukepighetti It's quite obvious that the team is not going to support SVG, but will provide support for vector images. Like that it don't need to struggle with the complexity of full SVG spec and keep going with a perfect image-only vector file format. Could be like a lite-SVG format.

I don't really get why we need to design a new vector file format. Surely there must be something out there that meets our needs? 

I mean, let's say we design something super awesome and bespoke, we still won't be able to get those assets out of design software like Figma. We'd need some kind of translator. At that point we might as well just come up with some kind of `flutter translate myfile.svg` into CustomPainter classes, which there are already examples of.

That said, the four major export formats for image assets are `png`, `jpg`, `svg`, `pdf`. So if we're not going to support `svg` then we can probably pick whatever we want as long as there's a way to get there easily from `svg` via a translator.

Unless, of course, I am completely missing the point here, which is possible.

@lukepighetti I don't think you're missing the point, this issue reeks of NIH syndrome. SVG is probably the most widely accepted and supported vector format in existence. Lacking support for it in a framework intended for mobile app development is arguably worse than lacking support for JPEG.
NIH syndrome doesn't even explain it, since we have [google/iconvg](https://github.com/google/iconvg) as a contender, which is in house goog and appears to have SVG translators already available
My prediction is this is going to get kicked out for months/years and everyone will move on and @dnfield will prop vector graphics on the platform up with `flutter_svg`

@lukepighetti 
> I don't really get why we need to design a new vector file format. Surely there must be something out there that meets our needs?

I do not know why it's called "new" now, but the engineering constraints demand compact binary format. Initially this issue was about exposing skia's vector drawing capabilities without bloating the engine, ie (as I envisioned a couple years ago) on the engine side a small rudimentary interpreter of bytecode should be enough. 

> my prediction is this is going to get kicked out for months/years

Sad, but apparently true. 64 months passed, and counting. Probably we do not know of some internal policy issue forbidding flutter team from adding fast drawing capabilities to the static assets domain.

@rmtmckenzie 
> if a completely new vector format on its own is going to help with

Both SVG and the subsed called VectorDravable are XML based. We need not to parse ML trees in the engine. That's left for converter tools.

We wouldn't want to expose the internal Skia format because that format changes all the time. If we're going to expose something that is longer-lived than that, then we need to design it very carefully. I have a work-in-progress document that goes through the requirements we'd need to do that, I should make it available sometime.
Is IconVG still a candidate or has some divergent design goal with it been identified?

I think supporting IconVG for Flutter is a great idea. I'm not quite sure it would resolve this issue, due to the narrowness of its scope. Another current challenge would be writing a backend for it that doesn't depend on the Go runtime. Another comparison to consider when looking at how it would do rendering the Material Design icons is against the _fonts_ we use to ship them, that is really how Flutter apps consume Material Design icons (not as SVGs). It would probably be possible to support IconVG as part of flutter_svg, or using the same architecture as flutter_svg - i.e. without any changes to the engine.

The basic idea of this issue is that existing vector graphic formats (PDF, PostScript, SVG as front-runners) don't quite measure up to what we want. They're either overly-complex and expanisve (PDF includes a 3d modeling API; SVG includes basically the entire CSS spec and JavaScript; PostScript is also a programming language). You end up wanting at best a subset of their functionality, even then you end up with baggage. `flutter_svg` only supports a narrow subset of the spec, but still needs a full XML parser. I made some choices to make sure we wouldn't need a full in-memory DOM for it, but that still means it's really hard to support some features people want. On top of that, there's now SVG 2.0, which isn't widely adopted, and has added new features without resolving some of these issues - which perhaps aren't issues it even cares to resolve.

So we have choices to make between some tactical solutions (limited SVG support, perhaps full IconVG support). But this issue is really about coming up with a longer-term solution, which involves some hard work and planning. 

Flutter may also still want something even more immediate, e.g. something that can basically dump out shaders and/or pipeline state information. For instance, it'd be fantastic to be able to just use Chrome to render the SVG and gather some platform independent shader/state information from that and have Flutter render that.

Is the point of this issue to create a brand new vector format that solves the issues of existing formats which could then be used outside of Flutter?

If not, can we make a binary format that maps to imperative CustomPainter code?

> Is the point of this issue to create a brand new vector format that solves the issues of existing formats which could then be used outside of Flutter?

Yes.

> can we make a binary format that maps to imperative CustomPainter code?

Certainly; that would be best done as a package and not as part of the core framework or engine (same as flutter_svg).
If the goal of this issue is to solve vector graphics for the world, optionally consuming the result in Flutter, perhaps it deserves a larger home than a single issue on `flutter/flutter` repo?
In due course, yup.

> I think supporting IconVG for Flutter is a great idea. I'm not quite sure it would resolve this issue, due to the narrowness of its scope. Another current challenge would be writing a backend for it that doesn't depend on the Go runtime.

Some of you might already have noticed IconVG's new home: https://github.com/google/iconvg

**There is now an IconVG decoder written in C**. Pluggable rasterizer back-ends include Cairo and Skia. It's about 3.6k lines of code. In comparison, e.g. [librsvg](https://gitlab.gnome.org/GNOME/librsvg) is about 35.9k lines or code, not counting its numerous dependencies.

That C library is hot off the presses, not even a version 0.1 yet, but it should be substantial enough for anyone here to play with it if motivated.

There's also a couple of issues filed for [discussing IconVG animation](https://github.com/google/iconvg/issues/2) and some consequent implications for [the IconVG wire format](https://github.com/google/iconvg/issues/4).
just FYI, there is interesting SVG rasterizer https://github.com/RazrFalcon/resvg which preprocess SVG into simplified Micro SVG form https://github.com/RazrFalcon/resvg/tree/master/usvg

> That C library is hot off the presses, not even a version 0.1 yet, but it should be substantial enough for anyone here to play with it if motivated.

For anyone playing with it, please sync past https://github.com/google/iconvg/commit/09a6ed3813202ef0601e1d222f7f8c80247fcfd6 (pushed a few hours ago) to pick up a bug fix in the "convert IconVG to PNG" program. How embarrassing!

> Another comparison to consider when looking at how it would do rendering the Material Design icons is against the fonts we use to ship them, that is really how Flutter apps consume Material Design icons (not as SVGs).

I forgot to mention... IIUC such icon fonts can typically only represent single color icons (e.g. black, with alpha). This works for the Material Design icons, but in general, vector graphics can obviously be multi-colored and IconVG can represent that.

OpenType fonts can admittedly represent colorful emoji, but there are multiple ways to do this (at the file format level) and sometimes this is done by [embedding SVG files](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/typography/opentype/spec/svg) in the TTF/OTF file, which obviously doesn't help here.

Such fonts could use [the COLR/CPAL tables](https://docs.microsoft.com/en-us/typography/opentype/spec/otff#tables-related-to-color-fonts) instead, I guess. Freetype only started supporting these tables in version 2.10 (released March 2019).
